name: Italian Game
description: A classical open game where White develops rapidly and fights for central control while aiming for flexible kingside activity.
ideas: Pressure the e5 and f7 squares with quick piece development
Prepare d4 or a later kingside attack after solid setup
Keep the bishop on c4 active against the black king
commonMistakes: Playing too slowly and allowing Black to equalize comfortably
Pushing d4 before White is fully prepared to recapture
Ignoring tactical ideas on f2 and c3 when the center opens

name: London System
description: A dependable setup for White built around dark-square control, easy development, and a resilient pawn structure.
ideas: Build a compact center and develop quickly without deep theory
Use the bishop pair and knight jumps to pressure e5 and c5
Transition smoothly into a kingside attack or minority-style play
commonMistakes: Letting the dark-squared bishop get traded without a reason
Neglecting central breaks with c4 or e4 when the position calls for them
Overcommitting on the kingside before finishing development

name: French Defense
description: Black challenges White's center from a solid structure and looks to strike back against the d4 and e4 pawns.
ideas: Undermine White's center with c5 and timely piece pressure
Use the e4 target to restrict White's space advantage
Develop patiently before counterattacking on the queenside
commonMistakes: Locking the position without a clear break plan
Delaying c5 and allowing White to keep a free space edge
Misplacing the light-squared bishop where it has no future

name: Sicilian Defense
description: A sharp counterattacking response to e4 where Black fights for asymmetry and active piece play from move one.
ideas: Contest d4 and build queenside play with ...a6 and ...b5 ideas
Use the c-file and central pressure to challenge White's initiative
Create imbalanced positions that reward active calculation
commonMistakes: Ignoring development while chasing flank pawn moves
Allowing White an easy e5 push without counterplay
Forgetting tactical shots on d6, b5, or e6 in open Sicilian structures

name: Queen's Gambit
description: A strategic opening where White offers the c-pawn to challenge the center and build long-term queenside pressure.
ideas: Use the c4 pawn break to question Black's d5 center
Develop harmoniously and pressure c- and e-file weaknesses
Convert space and piece activity into a favorable middlegame
commonMistakes: Recovering the gambit pawn too eagerly and losing activity
Leaving the queen's bishop blocked for too long
